one_line_takeaway: Using kernel ridge regression as an exact proxy for wide neural networks, the paper proves that clustered dirty-label backdoor poisons can be provably invisible to spectral/curvature-based detectors in a 'near-clone' regime, and that input-gradient regularization mitigates this only by trading off data-fitting capacity, an unavoidable safety-efficacy trade-off confirmed on CIFAR-10/100 ResNets.
key_finding: For the exponential kernel in the near-clone regime, poison efficacy (score shift) remains order-one/Theta(m) while the induced input-Hessian/Fisher curvature spike vanishes quadratically (Lambda_GN ~ r^2/ell^4), which the paper argues is a provable, not merely empirical, mechanism for spectrally-undetectable backdoors -- and Neural Collapse dynamics push triggered examples of any class toward the target class's feature mean, placing real dirty-label poisons in exactly this near-clone regime (evidenced via CIFAR-10 PCA of input vs. pre-fc features and per-class feature statistics at theta=0.01, Table 3/Appendix B). Empirically on deep ResNets there is a consistent detectability lag: ASR rises with poison fraction theta well before the poison-direction/top-eigenvector cosine overlap becomes non-trivial, across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, both poison types, and both stochastic/deterministic settings (Figures 6,7,9,11,13). Input-gradient regularization (kappa) reduces ASR but always at a monotonic cost to clean test accuracy, confirming an unavoidable safety-efficacy trade-off; the effect is not simply capacity loss -- for the deterministic CIFAR-100 case, increasing kappa causes the poison direction to rotate among different Hessian eigenvector indices rather than being fully suppressed (Table 2: max-overlap eigenvector index shifts, e.g., from index 0 at kappa=0,theta=0.01 to indices 1-2 at kappa=10^4 with generally increasing max overlap magnitude as theta grows). Increasing training duration (90 to 450 epochs) improves the accuracy side of the Pareto frontier but does not eliminate the trade-off and can even boost previously-suppressed poison efficacy: at theta=0.02, kappa=10^5 on CIFAR-100 (Table 1), going from 90 to 450 epochs raises accuracy only modestly (75.17% to 79.05%) while ASR jumps from 6.63% to 70.68%. Data augmentation combined with gradient regularization is much more effective than either alone: without augmentation the poisoned Hessian eigenvector shows the trigger in the identical spatial location (Fig. 8a-c), whereas with augmentation the trigger signature becomes spatially inconsistent/absent for high-kappa cases (Fig. 8d,h), and the paper reports this joint approach yields 'the first known neural networks which are immune to data poisoning' within their tested regime (Section 6 conclusion).
core_contribution: The paper develops a theoretical framework, using kernel ridge regression (KRR) as an exact model of wide neural networks, for how clustered dirty-label data-poisoning/backdoor attacks manifest geometrically in input space. It proves that a clustered poison cluster induces a rank-one spike in the input Hessian (via the Gauss-Newton term) whose magnitude scales quadratically with attack efficacy (score shift), giving a spike-efficacy law Lambda_GN(x0) = R_k(x0,zeta) * (Delta f(x0))^2. For nonlinear (exponential) kernels it identifies a 'near-clone' regime (poison point x0 within distance r << length-scale ell of the poison cluster centroid zeta) in which efficacy stays order-one while induced curvature vanishes quadratically (R_k = r^2/ell^4 -> 0), making such poisons provably undetectable to spectral/curvature-based defenses; for linear kernels R_k is order-one and no such lag exists. The paper then proposes and analyzes a defense: adding an input-gradient-norm penalty (kappa/2 * E||grad_x L||^2) to training. It proves (i) this provably and monotonically reduces effective degrees of freedom / data-fitting capacity (Theorem 3.9/A.5), (ii) under gradient flow it exponentially contracts high-energy (poison-aligned) input-Fisher eigenmodes (Theorem 3.12/A.6), and (iii) for exponential kernels the regularization is equivalent to an anisotropic high-pass filter that increases the effective length-scale (ell_eff^2 = ell^2 + c*kappa), shrinking the near-clone regime and thus the range over which poisons can hide. It links the near-clone assumption empirically to Neural Collapse (poisoned/triggered examples of any class get pulled toward the target class's feature mean, i.e., become near-clones in feature space even while remaining visually indistinguishable in input space). It validates the theory on linear regression (MNIST square-trigger poison, QR-decomposition weight-update analysis matching SGD retraining) and on deep PreResNet-110 trained from scratch on CIFAR-10/100 with two poison types (rank-1 additive/L-shaped trigger a la BadNets, and WaNet imperceptible warp poison), under stochastic and deterministic poisoning, with and without data augmentation, and across training durations (90 vs 450 epochs).
threat_model: Attacker controls a fraction theta of the training set and inserts a small, clustered set of dirty-label (mislabelled, trigger-marked) poison samples ('clustered dirty-label' / BadNets-style rank-1 additive triggers and WaNet-style imperceptible warp triggers) to induce a backdoor: a model that behaves normally on clean inputs but predicts an attacker-chosen label when the trigger is present. Two defender access variants are considered: 'Stochastic Poison' (defender/analyst has only clean data and the already-poisoned trained model) and 'Deterministic Poison' (defender has access to the poisoned training data itself and the poisoned model). Defenses considered are spectral/curvature-based detection (inspecting the top eigenvector of the input Hessian / input Fisher for alignment with the poison direction) and input-gradient-norm regularization as a training-time mitigation. The paper does not consider adaptive attackers who know the specific defense/regularization strength being used, nor multi-trigger or non-clustered poisoning strategies.
limitations: The core theory rests on Assumption 3.1 (tight poison cluster with negligible cross-block effects dominating the score/gradient at the trigger point), which is an approximation whose fidelity to real dirty-label poisoning is only justified indirectly via the Neural Collapse argument and CIFAR-10 PCA/feature-statistics evidence, not derived from first principles for deep nets. The authors explicitly note they 'do not quantify to what extent the deviation [in intra-class variance for poisoned vs. clean classes] impacts our theoretical analysis,' flagging it as future work. The KRR-as-exact-model-of-wide-neural-networks framing is an idealization (infinite-width/NTK-style regime) whose match to the finite-width PreResNet-110 experiments is empirical/qualitative rather than proven. The paper itself observes that in the deterministic-poisoning case the ordering of spectral markers with kappa is 'no longer monotonic' and that the poison appears to rotate among multiple Hessian eigenvectors rather than being cleanly suppressed, complicating the simple 'regularization suppresses the top eigenvector' detection story. Increased training epochs are shown to close the accuracy/robustness gap in the attacker's favor in at least one measured configuration (Table 1), suggesting the proposed defense's benefits are sensitive to training duration in ways not fully characterized. The threat model does not consider an adaptive attacker aware of the specific gradient-regularization defense being deployed, nor poisoning strategies beyond clustered/rank-one and WaNet-style triggers.

quantitative_results: MNIST linear/logistic regression poisoning: regression-to-argmax classification accuracy ~85% vs logistic regression ~90%

CIFAR-100 PreResNet-110, theta=0.02, augmentation=True (Table 1): at kappa=10^4, 90 epochs -> Acc 80.85%, ASR 82.10%; 450 epochs -> Acc 84.19%, ASR 88.18%. At kappa=3x10^4, 90 epochs -> Acc 79.18%, ASR 71.96%; 450 epochs -> Acc 82.28%, ASR 81.99%. At kappa=10^5, 90 epochs -> Acc 75.17%, ASR 6.63%; 450 epochs -> Acc 79.05%, ASR 70.68% (a ~2% accuracy gain accompanies a jump from near-ineffective to ~70% ASR)

CIFAR-10 non-stochastic (deterministic) poison, max cosine-overlap-squared with top Hessian eigenvectors across (kappa, theta) (Table 2): kappa=0,theta=0.01 -> overlap 0.4956 (index 0); kappa=0,theta=0.02 -> 0.3765 (index 1); kappa=0,theta=0.03 -> 0.4999 (index 0); kappa=10^4,theta=0.008 -> 0.6666 (index 2); kappa=10^4,theta=0.009 -> 0.7904 (index 1); kappa=10^4,theta=0.01 -> 0.9363 (index 1); kappa=10^4,theta=0.02 -> 0.9463 (index 0)

Spike-efficacy law derived analytically: input-Hessian top eigenvalue Lambda_GN(x0) = R_k(x0,zeta) * (Delta f(x0))^2, i.e., curvature spike scales quadratically in poison efficacy while efficacy itself grows only linearly (then saturates) in poison count m
For exponential kernel in near-clone regime (r << ell): R_k(x0,zeta) = r^2/ell^4 -> 0, so curvature vanishes quadratically in r/ell while efficacy Delta f(x0) remains order-one
baselines_compared: kappa=0 (no gradient regularization) as the no-defense baseline across all CIFAR-10/100 sweeps
QR stepwise weight-update prediction vs. full SGD retraining-from-scratch (MNIST linear regression, shown to be a near-perfect match)
No-augmentation vs. data-augmentation training regimes, compared for their effect on ASR/accuracy/spectral visibility
Stochastic vs. deterministic poisoning threat models compared for consistency of spectral-detectability lag
BadNets-style rank-1 additive/L-shaped trigger poisoning vs. WaNet-style imperceptible warp poisoning as two distinct attack variants
